claim: In-situ synchrotron high-energy X-ray diffraction (HEXRD) and high-resolution microscopic digital image correlation (μ-DIC) are employed to study the evolution of stress and strain in different phases upon loading.
evidence_text: employing various in-situ characterization techniques including synchrotron high-energy X-ray diffraction and high-resolution microscopic digital image correlation to study the evolution of stress/strain in different phases upon loading.

claim: Adding Al up to 12 wt.% in medium Mn steel reduces mass density, with 1 wt.% Al addition corresponding to approximately 1.2% reduction in mass density.
evidence_text: medium manganese (Mn) lightweight steel with Al additions (up to 12 wt.% [2,3]) that serve to reduce the density of the material (1 wt.% Al reduces ~1.2 % mass density [3,4])

claim: Depending on the stacking fault energy (SFE), retained austenite may twin upon deformation, producing a twinning-induced plasticity (TWIP) effect that enhances strain hardening through dynamic Hall-Petch effect and back stress hardening from dislocation accumulation at twin boundaries.
evidence_text: Depending on the stacking fault energy (SFE), the retained austenite may also be twinned upon deformation, resulting in an additional strain-hardening effect known as the twinning-induced plasticity (TWIP). This strain-hardening mechanism is due to the continuous formation of deformation twins that results in the ‘dynamic Hall-Petch effect’ and a back stress hardening caused by the accumulation of dislocations at the twin boundaries [21–23].

claim: In duplex stainless steels and conventional low-alloy TRIP steels, yield strength along the transverse direction (TD) is higher than along the rolling direction (RD), often attributed to crystallographic textures developed during rolling.
evidence_text: the yield strength along the transverse direction (TD) is higher than that along the rolling direction (RD) [36,37,39]. This was often attributed to the crystallographic textures that were developed during the rolling processes [38,39].

claim: Deformation anisotropy in medium Mn lightweight steels is more complex than in other multiphase steels because of more phase constituents, their complicated interactions, and the occurrence of deformation-induced martensite that influences strain hardening and strain/stress patterns.
evidence_text: the deformation anisotropy can be more complex in medium lightweight Mn steels. Such complexity arises from a) the existence of more types of phase constituents and their more complicated interactions as a response to loading, and b) the occurrence of deformation-induced martensite that can significantly influence the steels’ strain-hardening capability and strain/stess patterning developed during loading.

claim: The mass density of this steel is calculated to be ~7.40 g/cm³ based on equations proposed in Ref. [40].
evidence_text: Its mass density was calculated to be ~7.40 g/ cm3 based on equations proposed in Ref. [40].

claim: The ingots were homogenized at 1100°C before hot rolling.
evidence_text: The ingots were prepared in a vacuum induction furnace, and then homogenized at 1100°C before being hot-rolled to a thickness of ~3 mm.

claim: After homogenization, the steel was hot-rolled to a thickness of ~3 mm, and the temperature was maintained above ~1000°C during hot rolling.
evidence_text: hot-rolled to a thickness of ~3 mm. The temperature was maintained above ~1000°C during hot rolling.

claim: The cold-rolled sheet was subjected to intercritical annealing at 850°C for 10 minutes, followed by water quenching to room temperature, to produce a microstructure containing ultrafine γ+α aggregate and δ ferrite.
evidence_text: To produce the desired microstructure containing ultrafine γ+α aggregate and δ ferrite, the cold-rolled sheet was subjected to IA at 850°C for 10 minutes, followed by water quenching to room temperature.

claim: In-situ synchrotron HEXRD experiments were conducted at beamline P02.1 of PETRA III, DESY, with a fixed energy of ~60 keV (wavelength ~0.20738 Å) and a beam size of 0.5×0.5 mm².
evidence_text: The beamline was operated at a fixed energy of ~ 60 keV, corresponding to a monochromatic X-ray with a wavelength of ~0.20738 Å. The beam size is 0.5 × 0.5 mm^2.

claim: Quasi-static uniaxial tensile testing was conducted with a constant crosshead speed corresponding to an initial strain rate of ~1×10⁻³ s⁻¹.
evidence_text: quasi-static uniaxial tensile testing was conducted simultaneously using a Kammrath & Weiss stress rig, which maintained a constant crosshead speed corresponding to an initial strain rate of ~ 1 × 1 0 − 3 s−1.

claim: The detector distance and instrument broadening were calibrated using a standard CeO₂ sample.
evidence_text: The detector distance and instrument broadening were calibrated by a standard CeO2 sample.

claim: Diffraction intensities over the entire azimuth range (0°–360°) were integrated using the Fit2D software.
evidence_text: The diffraction intensities over the entire azimuth range (0°–360°) were integrated using the Fit2D software [42].

claim: For lattice strain analysis, the Debye-Scherrer diffraction ring was divided into 36 sectors of 10° each along the azimuth angle, and the sector along the tensile direction (azimuth angle from -5° to 5°) was selected.
evidence_text: To analyze the lattice strain of each phase and its change upon loading, the Debye-Scherrer diffraction ring was divided into 36 sectors (10° each) along the azimuth angle and the sector along the tensile direction (azimuth angle from -5° to 5°) was selected for such analysis.

claim: Lattice strain ε_hkl was calculated as (d_hkl^σ - d_hkl^0)/d_hkl^0, and the weighted average lattice strain per phase ε_p as (average a_p^σ - average a_p^0)/average a_p^0, where d_hkl^σ and d_hkl^0 are interplanar spacings under load and unloaded, and average a_p^σ, average a_p^0 are the weighted average lattice parameters for loaded and unloaded samples.
evidence_text: where d_hkl^σ and d_hkl^0 are the interplanar spacings of the sample under tensile load and prior to deformation, respectively; average a_p^σ and average a_p^0 are the weighted average lattice parameters of each phase for the loaded and unloaded samples, respectively.

claim: The non-deformed microstructure exhibits a banded or layered structure with ultrafine-grained γ+α aggregates and coarse δ-ferrite (grain size above ~15 μm) aligned along RD.
evidence_text: The sample exhibits a banded or layered structure, featuring ultrafinegrained (UFG) γ+α phases that have reversely transformed from martensite during annealing (Fig. 3(a-c)), and a certain fraction of coarse-grained δ-ferrite (grain size above ~15 μm). The band direction is along RD.

claim: The average grain sizes are ~1.8 μm for austenite, ~2 μm for α-ferrite, and ~38 μm for δ-ferrite.
evidence_text: the average grain size can be calculated, which is ~1.8 μm, ~2 μm, and ~38 μm for austenite, α-ferrite, and δ-ferrite, respectively.

claim: The volume fractions are 25 vol.% δ-ferrite and 44 vol.% α-ferrite, resulting in an estimated 56 vol.% for the γ+α aggregate.
evidence_text: which are 25 vol. % and 44 vol. %, respectively. The fraction of γ+α aggregate is thus estimated to be 56 vol. %.

claim: The intercritically annealed steel achieves a tensile strength of ~855±35 MPa and total elongation of 65±5%.
evidence_text: The intercritically annealed steel demonstrates a great combination of tensile strength (~855±35 MPa) and ductility (total elongation 65±5 %).

claim: The strain-hardening rate curve shows three stages: a sharp drop in Stage I, an increase starting from a true strain of ~0.2 in Stage II, and a decrease in Stage III until necking.
evidence_text: The strain-hardening behavior ... can be categorized into three distinct stages. At stage I, the strain hardening rate drops sharply. Stage II is characterized by an increase in strain-hardening rate, starting from a true strain of ~0.2 for both samples. As the strain further increases, the strain-hardening rate begins to decrease again, i.e., Stage III, until the occurrence of necking.

claim: In the TD sample strained to 15%, the band slope (BS) values of the BCC phase exhibit a bimodal distribution that distinguishes ferrite from deformation-induced α'-martensite.
evidence_text: Fig. 5(b) presents a bimodal distribution of the BS value of the BCC phase obtained using the AZtecCrystal software. This information helps to differentiate ferrite and α’-martensite.

claim: Strain-induced α'-martensite nucleates primarily at γ/α and γ/δ phase boundaries, driven by high stress and strain concentrations at heterointerfaces.
evidence_text: α’-martensite nucleation takes place primarily at the phase boundary between austenite and ferrite (α-ferrite or δ-ferrite), as marked by the arrows in Fig. 5(d). This preferable nucleation is due to the large stress and strain concentration developed at the heterointerfaces.

claim: TEM analysis confirms that strain-induced α'-martensite nucleates at or close to γ/α and γ/δ phase boundaries, aided by high local strain/stress concentration and interfacial defects such as ledges that promote activation of multiple slip systems.
evidence_text: The results confirm that strain-induced a’- martensite is nucleated at or close to γ/α and γ/δ phase boundaries. ... high strain and stress concentrations ... some defects such as ledges at interfaces can serve as favorable sites for the activation of multiple slip systems ... result in more frequent intersections between different microscopic shear-bands ... facilitate the nucleation of a’-martensite.

claim: In austenite grains without α'-martensite, dislocation planar slip and dense stacking faults are observed, whereas both α and δ ferrite grains contain dislocation tangles indicative of cross-slip.
evidence_text: Dislocation planar slip and dense stacking faults (SFs, marked by the red arrows) are observed in these grains ... TEM observation taken from the two-beam condition (g vector <110>) reveals the formation of dislocation tangles (Fig. 7(b) and (c)) inside both α and δ ferrite, indicating the occurrence of dislocation cross-slip.

claim: The difference in deformation mode between austenite (planar slip, strain-induced martensite) and ferrite (dislocation tangles, cross-slip) is attributed to the lower stacking fault energy of austenite compared to ferrite.
evidence_text: Austenite generally possesses a lower SFE than the ferrite phase, which favors planar dislocation slip and the occurrence of strain-induced martensite.

claim: High-resolution microscopic digital image correlation (μ-DIC) was performed on RD and TD samples within a SEM using nano-scale gold speckles to correlate local strain with phases identified by prior EBSD.
evidence_text: To investigate the strain partitioning between different phases during the deformation process, high-resolution μ-DIC tests were performed on the steel samples with two different orientations (RD and TD). ... Nano-scale gold speckles were applied to the sample through gold sputtering and gold film remodeling. Secondary electron imaging, covering a total area of 500×500 μm², was performed at different interrupted strains.

claim: High strain and stress concentrations at γ/α and γ/δ interfaces and interfacial defects such as ledges serve as favorable sites for multiple slip, promoting intersections of shear bands and facilitating α'-martensite nucleation.
evidence_text: First, the high strain and stress concentrations at the interface regions, resulting from the strain incapability between adjacent phases, enhance dislocation activities and thus the interaction between different slipping systems close to these local regions. Second, some defects such as ledges at interfaces can serve as favorable sites for the activation of multiple slip systems.

claim: In austenite grains where α'-martensite has not yet formed, dislocation planar slip and dense stacking faults are observed (Fig.7(a)).
evidence_text: For some other austenite grains, α’-martensite has not yet formed (Fig.7(a)). Dislocation planar slip and dense stacking faults (SFs, marked by the red arrows) are observed in these grains

claim: TEM observations under two-beam condition reveal dislocation tangles inside both α and δ ferrite, indicating the occurrence of dislocation cross-slip.
evidence_text: TEM observation taken from the two-beam condition (g vector <110>) reveals the formation of dislocation tangles (Fig.7(b) and (c)) inside both α and δ ferrite, indicating the occurrence of dislocation cross-slip.

claim: At high strain levels above ~0.22 true strain, the BCC peaks of the RD sample are clearly higher than those of the TD sample, indicating a higher amount of martensite formation in RD.
evidence_text: At high strain levels (e. g., above ~0.22), the BCC peaks of the RD sample appear to be clearly higher than that of the TD sample, indicating a higher amount of martensite formation.

claim: The martensite fraction is negligible (less than 1 vol%) at the elastic stage (true strain below ~0.03), confirming that transformation occurs primarily during plastic deformation.
evidence_text: At the elastic stage (true strain below ~0.03), the martensite fraction is negligible (less than 1 vol. %), suggesting that the martensite transformation in the studied steel occurs primarily during the plastic deformation stages (i.e., strain-induced α’-martensite).

claim: The RD sample exhibits higher martensite transformation kinetics than the TD sample, and the difference in martensite fraction reaches as high as 4 vol% at a true strain of ~0.48 (the uniform elongation of TD).
evidence_text: A notable feature is that the RD sample shows higher martensite transformation kinetics compared with the TD sample. At higher strain levels, the difference in the martensite fraction between the two samples becomes more pronounced, which can be as high as 4 vol. % at a true strain of ~0.48 (the uniform elongation of the TD sample).

claim: The faster martensite transformation kinetics in the RD sample can explain its higher strain hardening ability, especially at high strain levels above ~0.2 true strain.
evidence_text: The faster martensite transformation kinetics in the RD sample can explain its higher strain hardening ability, particularly at high strain levels (above ~0.2 true strain, see the inset in Fig. 8(b)).

claim: During tensile deformation, the RD sample exhibits a significantly higher dislocation density in austenite than the TD sample, with a maximum difference of ~1.51×10^14 m^{-2}.
evidence_text: During deformation, the dislocation density of austenite is obviously higher in the RD sample (by a maximum value of ~1.51 × 10^14 m^{-2}).

claim: The higher dislocation density in austenite of the RD sample is associated with strain-induced α'-martensitic transformation: the volume expansion generates localized plasticity, and the mechanical contrast among ferrite, austenite and martensite induces dislocation accommodation in austenite.
evidence_text: This higher dislocation may be associated with strain-induced α’-martensitic transformation. On the one hand, the volume expansion associated with the martensitic phase transformation generates localized plasticity in the matrix phase adjacent to the newly formed martensite, leading to an enhancement in dislocation activity within the matrix. On the other hand, the mechanical contrast among ferrite, austenite and fresh martensite results in distinct stress/strain partitioning, which consequently induces dislocation accommodation in softer phases (here austenite).

claim: At true strains above 0.35, the difference in austenite dislocation density between RD and TD becomes smaller due to martensite transformation of high-dislocation-density austenite; this decay in dislocation multiplication reduces the strain hardening rate, so the difference in dislocation multiplication is not the reason for the hardening difference.
evidence_text: At the latest stages of tensile deformation (above 0.35 true strain), the difference in the austenite dislocation density between the two samples becomes smaller, which might be due to the eventual martensite transformation from the austenite with a high dislocation density. Such decay in the rate of dislocation multiplication, particularly for the RD sample, will lead to a decrease in the strain hardening rate... Therefore, the difference in the dislocation multiplication between the two samples should not be the reason accounting for their different strain-hardening ability.

claim: TEM observation shows strain-induced α'-martensite nucleates close to γ/α and γ/δ phase boundaries in the TD sample deformed to 15% engineering strain.
evidence_text: Fig. 6. TEM observation of austenite deformation mechanisms in the TD sample (engineering strain 15%): … showing the strain-induced α’-martensite nucleation close to γ/α and γ/δ phase boundaries.

claim: In the TD sample deformed to 15% engineering strain, austenite contains stacking faults, while δ-ferrite and α-ferrite exhibit dislocation tangles.
evidence_text: Fig. 7. TEM observation of the deformation behavior within austenite, α-ferrite and δ-ferrite for the TD sample (engineering strain 15%): (a) … showing the formation of stacking fault … (b) … showing the dislocation tangles … (c) … showing the dislocation tangles.

claim: Strain partitioning was investigated by in-situ μ-DIC with interrupted tensile testing, acquiring SEM images at the same locations at every ~5% engineering strain; correlative EBSD was performed before gold sputtering, so the analysis does not distinguish strain-induced α'-martensite from austenite.
evidence_text: interrupted tensile testing. SEM images at same locations of the tensile specimen were performed at every ~5 % engineering strain. It should be noted that the correlative EBSD results were acquired before gold sputtering, thus the analysis does not distinguish strain-induced α’-martensite and austenite.

claim: In the RD sample, the strain distribution across the entire region is relatively uniform, indicating that δ-ferrite and UFG γ+α aggregates deform similarly up to 20% engineering strain, except for some deformation slip bands appearing at 20% strain.
evidence_text: The strain distribution is relatively uniform throughout the entire region, indicating that the δ-ferrite and UFG γ+α exhibit similar deformation levels … At higher strain values, e.g., 20 %, some deformation slip bands can be observed

claim: The TD sample exhibits a more pronounced strain heterogeneity than the RD sample, already visible at lower magnification, with higher strain concentrated in the δ-phase.
evidence_text: In contrast to the RD sample, the TD sample demonstrates a more pronounced strain heterogeneity, which can be already observed at a lower magnification (Fig. 11(a)). A detailed strain profile analysis reveals that the higher strain is concentrated in the δ-phase

claim: The strain distribution in the UFG γ+α region of the TD sample closely resembles that observed in the RD sample, but within the TD sample, the γ-α mixed phase region shows relatively higher strain compared to the α-ferrite region.
evidence_text: The strain distribution in the UFG γ+α region of the TD sample closely resembles that observed in the RD sample. Fig. 11(c) and (d) illustrate … revealing a relatively higher strain inside the γ-α mixed

claim: The volume expansion associated with the γ to α'-martensitic transformation (around 2~4%) may contribute to increased local plastic strain in the γ-α' mixed phase, but the strain partitioning between α and γ-α' is already notable at small strains (5%) where martensite formation is limited, indicating that the observed strain concentration is more due to mechanical contrast between the two phases.
evidence_text: It should be noted that the volume expansion associated with the γ to α’-martensitic transformation (around 2~4 % [77]) is also likely to contribute to an increase in local plastic strain in the γ-α’ mixed phase [78]. However, the strain partitioning between α and γ-α' is already notable at small strains (5 %) where martensite formation is relatively limited. This indicates that the observed strain concentration within the γ-α’ mixed phase is more due to the mechanical contrast between the two phases.

claim: When the layered microstructure is parallel to the loading direction (RD sample), δ-ferrite and γ+α aggregates deform under an iso-strain condition; when perpendicular (TD sample), they deform under an iso-stress condition leading to strain concentration in the softer δ-ferrite.
evidence_text: When the layered structure is aligned in parallel with the loading direction (RD sample), the two microstructure constitutes (δ-ferrite and γ+α aggregates) are more likely to be deformed in an iso-strain condition, based on plastic deformation theories of composite materials [86]. In contrast, for the TD sample where the layer direction is perpendicular to the loading direction, an iso-stress deformation condition is more prevailed [86].

claim: The plastic strain concentrated in the austenite phase for the RD sample is higher than that for the TD sample by a factor of ~1.15.
evidence_text: Results in Section 3.4 have shown that the plastic strain concentrated in the austenite phase for the RD sample is higher than that for the TD sample by a factor of ~1.15 (Fig. 12 (c) and (d)).

claim: Using the Olson-Cohen model with parameters α=1.35, β=2, n=2 and initial austenite fraction 46.5 vol.%, the martensite fraction of the RD sample was predicted by assuming ε_RD = 1.15 ε_TD, and the prediction matches HEXRD experimental data (Fig. 14).
evidence_text: Here, the constants α, β and n are closely related to the shear band formation rate and the SFE of austenite, and FγO is the initial austenite fraction (46.5 vol.%). The parameters used in the OC model equations are summarized in Table 1. It is assumed that martensite nucleation occurs randomly, with a value of n=2 [61]. We first apply the OC model to the TD sample (the plastic strain is ε_TD). Subsequently, the fitting parameters obtained from the TD sample are utilized to fit the RD sample, where the plastic strain of austenite is taken as ε_RD = 1.15 ε_TD (determined by comparing the micro-DIC data, Fig. 12(c) and (d)). Fig. 14 illustrates the accuracy of the OC model in predicting the martensite fraction, as compared with the HEXRD experimental data.
